source_lists.title: New Queer Cinema
source_lists.desc:
NEW QUEER CINEMA
By: Grey Daisies

"The term “New Queer Cinema” was coined by film critic B. Ruby Rich in several publications (including the British film journal Sight & Sound1 and her seminal Village Voice overview of the 1992 Sundance Film Festival) to describe the appearance of certain films at Sundance Film Festivals in the early 1990s that evinced a politicized stance towards queer culture.

These films were unapologetic in their frank look at sexuality and combined stylistic elements drawn from AIDS activist videos, avant-garde cinemas, and even Hollywood films. They eschewed “positive images” and “happy endings” in favor of more complexly queer musings on the nature of gender and sexuality. Critic Karl Soehnlein noted this trend in 1990, when he… more

source_lists.title: New Queer Cinema
source_lists.desc:
A survey of the films that influenced, represent, and inherited the legacy of, the New Queer Cinema.

“New Queer Cinema” (also called the “Queer New Wave”) was coined by scholar B. Ruby Rich and refers to a movement in independent filmmaking that consisted of politically charged, queer-themed films. Centered primarily in the US, UK, and Canada, the movement began in the late ‘80s and lasted through the mid ‘90s. Many figures in the New Queer Cinema emerged from antiracist, feminist, and queer AIDS activist groups. The anti-assimilationist and militant video activism of these collectives inspired NQC filmmakers to challenge previous mainstream representations of queer people. Instead of depicting simplistic, respectable, or supporting queer characters, NQC filmmakers opted for more nuanced and even morally dubious… more

source_lists.title: New Queer Cinema
source_lists.desc: All movies mentioned or referenced to in B. Rubi Rich’s book New Queer Cinema.

source_lists.title: New Queer Cinema
source_lists.desc:
The production of foreign, experimental, and documentary films that centered on queer issues eventually helped spark the production of gay and lesbian independent feature film production in America. [...] The films, many fueled by 1980s AIDS activism, engaged with concepts being formulated within queer theory, and collectively they became known as the New Queer Cinema (x). Spanning roughly 1990-1995. 

“New Queer Cinema produced complex work that didn't simply create new gay heroes as subjects. It dealt with the politics of representation, it ventured into transgressive themes [and] challenged simple ideas about victimhood and subjugation”

Todd Haynes

Movies are taken from this article, and best viewed in chronological order. 


Other reads :

>An introduction to B. Ruby Rich
>Movie movements that defined cinema: New Queer Cinema
>What’s Happening to ‘Queer’ Cinema in the LGBT Film Boom?
>Development of the New Queer Cinema Movement
>Tout Queer

source_lists.title: New Queer Cinema
source_lists.desc:
Naro Video Film School June 2019 - New Queer Cinema

Each month we choose 25 films on a topic to help you explore our dazzling, curated collection. The films rent for half-price during the entire month.

June is LGBT Pride Month! New Queer Cinema, coined by B. Ruby Rich, describes the emergence of LGBT films beginning in the late 1980s which often included political themes, a more honest view of LGBT lives, and an indie or genre-bending aesthetic. The movement inspired a wave of filmmakers, as well as mainstream Hollywood, to bring more of these stories to audiences around the world.
source_lists.owner: List by Naro Video @ ODU Libraries
